meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) electrostatics items. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e Ch. 18 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). 'Select two answers' items, figure-dependent items (dipole/square/field-line diagrams), and free-response/numeric items were skipped. A few items in a figure-based series were included only where the answer is figure-independent (e.g., a polarized neutral conductor has zero net charge; the field between parallel plates is uniform).

questions.stem: When a glass rod is rubbed against silk, which of the following statements is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Electrons are removed from the silk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electrons are removed from the rod.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protons are removed from the silk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protons are removed from the rod.

questions.explanation: Charging by friction transfers electrons, not protons (protons are bound in nuclei). Glass gives up electrons to silk, so electrons are removed from the rod, leaving the glass rod positively charged and the silk negatively charged.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (friction transfers electrons; glass becomes positive).

questions.stem: If objects X and Y attract each other, which of the following will be false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: X has positive charge and Y has negative charge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: X has negative charge and Y has positive charge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: X and Y both have positive charge.

choices.label: D
choices.text: X is neutral and Y has a charge.

questions.explanation: Attraction can occur between opposite charges or between a charged object and a neutral one (by polarization). Two objects with the same sign of charge always repel, so 'both positive' cannot produce attraction—that statement is false.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (like charges repel).

questions.stem: Suppose a positively charged object A is brought in contact with an uncharged object B in a closed system. What type of charge will be left on object B?
questions.explanation: Charging by contact (conduction) shares charge between the objects, so both end up with the same sign. Touching a positive object to a neutral one leaves the neutral object positively charged.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (conduction yields the same sign).

questions.stem: What will be the net charge on an object that attracts neutral pieces of paper but repels a negatively charged balloon?
questions.explanation: Repulsion only occurs between like charges, so repelling a negatively charged balloon means the object is also negative. (Attracting neutral paper happens for any charged object via polarization, so it doesn't distinguish the sign.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (repulsion requires like charge).

questions.stem: In an experiment, a student runs a comb through his hair several times and brings it close to small pieces of paper. Which of the following will he observe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pieces of paper repel the comb.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pieces of paper are attracted to the comb.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some pieces of paper are attracted and some repel the comb.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no attraction or repulsion between the pieces of paper and the comb.

questions.explanation: Running the comb through hair charges it. The charged comb polarizes the neutral paper (inducing opposite charge on the near side), so the paper is attracted to the comb.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (charged comb attracts neutral paper by polarization).

questions.stem: Some students experimenting with an uncharged metal sphere want to give the sphere a net charge of the same sign as a charged aluminum pie plate. Which of the following steps would accomplish this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bringing the pie plate close to, but not touching, the metal sphere, then moving the pie plate away.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bringing the pie plate close to, but not touching, the metal sphere, then momentarily touching a grounding wire to the metal sphere.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bringing the pie plate close to, but not touching, the metal sphere, then momentarily touching a grounding wire to the pie plate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Touching the pie plate to the metal sphere.

questions.explanation: Charging by conduction (direct contact) shares charge so the sphere ends up with the same sign as the pie plate. Induction with grounding (option B) would give the sphere the opposite sign, and simply approaching and withdrawing (option A) leaves it neutral.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (conduction gives same sign; induction+ground gives opposite).

questions.stem: When a charged balloon is brought closer to a neutral metal sphere, there is a redistribution of charges within the sphere. What is this phenomenon called?
questions.explanation: The separation of charge within a conductor caused by a nearby charged object—without any charge being transferred—is called polarization (charge induction).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim (figure reference removed; concept is figure-independent). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: A charged balloon is brought close to one side of an isolated neutral metal sphere, causing the charges in the sphere to redistribute. What is the net charge on the sphere?

choices.label: D
choices.text: It can be positive or negative depending on the material.

questions.explanation: Polarization only separates the existing charges within the isolated sphere; no charge is added or removed. The near side becomes one sign and the far side the opposite sign, but the net charge of the sphere remains zero.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. This item from a figure-based series is answerable without the figure (an isolated, polarized conductor has zero net charge). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: A positively charged rod is used to charge a sphere by induction. Which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sphere must be a conductor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sphere must be an insulator.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be connected to ground.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be already charged.

questions.explanation: Charging by induction requires charges that are free to move and be redistributed (and typically a momentary ground connection), which only happens in a conductor. An insulator can only be polarized locally, not charged by induction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (induction needs free charges, i.e., a conductor).

questions.stem: Consider the (uniform) electric field created by two large, parallel, oppositely charged metal plates. Where is the electric field strongest?

choices.label: C
choices.text: At the midpoint between the plates

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the same at all points between the plates.

questions.explanation: The field between two large parallel charged plates is uniform: it has the same magnitude and direction at every point in the region between them (ignoring edge effects).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Based on OpenStax Figure 18.59; the labeled points are unnecessary because the field between parallel plates is uniform. Choices generalized to describe locations. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: The electrostatic force between two point charges is F. What will the force be if the distance between them is halved (charges unchanged)?
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law gives F ∝ 1/r². Halving the separation multiplies the force by 1/(½)² = 4, so the new force is 4F.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (F ∝ 1/r²).

questions.stem: For two point charges with electrostatic force F between them, which of the following statements is false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If the charge of one of the particles is doubled and that of the second is unchanged, the force will become 2F.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If the charge of one of the particles is doubled and that of the second is halved, the force will remain F.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If the charge of both particles is doubled, the force will become 4F.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the above (all three statements are true).

questions.explanation: Coulomb's law gives F ∝ q₁q₂. Doubling one charge gives 2F (A true); doubling one and halving the other gives 2 × ½ = 1, so F is unchanged (B true); doubling both gives 2 × 2 = 4F (C true). All three are correct, so the false statement is 'none of the above.'
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (all three scaling statements satisfy F ∝ q₁q₂).

questions.stem: Two massive, positively charged particles are initially held a fixed distance apart. When they are moved farther apart, the magnitude of their mutual gravitational force changes by a factor of n. By what factor does the magnitude of their mutual electrostatic force change?
questions.explanation: Both Newton's law of gravitation and Coulomb's law follow an inverse-square dependence on distance (∝ 1/r²). So the same change in separation scales both forces by the same factor n.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (both forces are inverse-square in r).

questions.stem: Two particles with charges +2q and +q are separated by a distance r. The +2q particle produces an electric field E at the +q particle's location and exerts a force F on it. What is the electric field of the +q particle at the location of the +2q particle, and what force does it exert on the +2q particle?
questions.explanation: Field is proportional to the source charge, so the +q particle (half the charge of +2q) produces half the field, E/2, at the same distance. By Newton's third law, the force the +q particle exerts on the +2q particle is equal in magnitude to F.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (field ∝ charge; forces equal by Newton's third law).

questions.stem: Two particles with charges +2q and +q are separated by a distance r; the +2q particle exerts a force F on the +q particle. When the +q particle is replaced by a +3q particle (at the same location), what will be the electric field and the force from the +2q particle experienced by the +3q particle?
questions.explanation: The electric field created by the +2q particle depends only on that source and the distance, so it is unchanged at E. The force on the new particle is F = qE, and tripling the charge (to +3q) triples the force to 3F.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (field set by source; force ∝ test charge).

questions.stem: The direction of the electric field of a negative charge is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: inward (toward the charge) at all surrounding points

choices.label: B
choices.text: outward (away from the charge) at all surrounding points

choices.label: C
choices.text: inward on one side and outward on the other

questions.explanation: By convention, electric field lines point toward negative charges. The field of a negative point charge therefore points radially inward (toward the charge) at every surrounding point, independent of any test charge.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Options reworded from the source's test-charge phrasing to clearly describe field direction (single best answer preserved). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (field points toward a negative charge).

questions.stem: The force responsible for holding an atom together is:
questions.explanation: The attraction between the negatively charged electrons and the positively charged nucleus is electric (electrostatic). This electromagnetic force binds the atom; gravity is far too weak at atomic scales.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (electron-nucleus attraction is electric).

questions.stem: When a positively charged particle exerts an inward (attractive) force on another particle P, what will be the charge of P?
questions.explanation: An inward (attractive) force toward a positive charge means P is attracted to the positive particle. Since unlike charges attract, P must be negative.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (attraction to a positive charge ⇒ P is negative).
